title: Employment Act 2008
chapters: 20 sections. A focused CORRECTIVE Act with three pillars: (1) DISPUTE RESOLUTION — repeal of the failed EA 2002 statutory procedures and establishment of the ACAS Code 25% adjustment mechanism (ss.1-7); (2) NATIONAL MINIMUM WAGE ENFORCEMENT — strengthened enforcement framework (ss.8-12); (3) EMPLOYMENT AGENCY ENFORCEMENT — enhanced powers for the Employment Agency Standards Inspectorate (ss.13-18). Plus s.19: trade union membership amendment following ASLEF v UK. CRITICAL EVOLUTIONARY NOTE: This Act sits at the PIVOT POINT of UK dispute resolution law. It ended the failed experiment of statutory procedures (EA 2002) and RESTORED the principles-based approach (ACAS Code + Polkey) that the ERA 2025 now builds upon. Its NMW and agency enforcement provisions are being CONSOLIDATED into the Fair Work Agency (April 2026).

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — New Labour Government (Brown ministry), introduced in the House of Lords
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Dispute resolution provisions (ss.1-7): 6 April 2009. NMW enforcement (ss.8-12): phased 2009. Employment agency enforcement (ss.13-18): phased 2009. Trade union membership (s.19): 2009.

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 — Fair Work Agency consolidation
significantAmendments.effect: The ERA 2025 establishes the Fair Work Agency (FWA) from April 2026, which CONSOLIDATES enforcement functions across NMW, holiday pay, statutory payments, and employment agency standards. The separate enforcement regimes strengthened by the EA 2008 will be brought under a single coordinated body with enhanced inspection, investigation, and penalty powers. The ACAS Code mechanism (s.3) continues unchanged — the ERA 2025 does not replace the principles-based approach to dispute resolution.

precedingDocuments.title: Gibbons Review — 'A Review of Employment Dispute Resolution in Great Britain' (March 2007)
precedingDocuments.significance: The independent review by Michael Gibbons (Better Regulation Commission) that concluded the EA 2002 statutory dispute resolution procedures were a FAILURE. Recommended repeal in their entirety and replacement with a strengthened ACAS Code of Practice. The Government accepted all recommendations.

precedingDocuments.title: Government Consultation — 'Resolving Disputes in the Workplace' (March 2007)
precedingDocuments.significance: The public consultation following the Gibbons Review. Confirmed overwhelming support for repeal of the statutory procedures. Led directly to the Employment Bill 2007-08.

precedingDocuments.title: ASLEF v United Kingdom [2007] ECHR 184
precedingDocuments.significance: The ECHR ruled that a trade union's right to freedom of association (Article 11 ECHR) includes the right to EXCLUDE members whose political beliefs are incompatible with the union's values. Led directly to s.19 of the EA 2008 amending TULRCA 1992 to enable unions to expel members of political parties.

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd
leadingCases.principle: A dismissal can be unfair purely on PROCEDURAL grounds, even if the outcome would have been the same. However, compensation can be reduced to reflect the chance the employee would have been dismissed in any event (the 'Polkey reduction'). The EA 2002 partially reversed this principle by inserting s.98A ERA 1996 — which the EA 2008 then REPEALED, RESTORING Polkey.
leadingCases.heldText: In the case of a redundancy, the employer will normally not act reasonably unless the employer warns and consults affected employees, adopts a fair basis of selection, and takes such steps as may be reasonable to avoid or minimise redundancy.
leadingCases.significance: Polkey is the CENTRAL PRINCIPLE of UK procedural fairness in dismissal law. The EA 2002 disrupted it. The EA 2008 restored it. The ERA 2025 builds on it. Eileen must understand this principle — it governs every dismissal dispute.

leadingCases.name: ASLEF v United Kingdom
leadingCases.principle: A trade union has the right to expel members whose political beliefs or political party membership are incompatible with the union's objects, provided adequate safeguards exist. This does not breach Article 11 ECHR (freedom of association).
leadingCases.heldText: The union's right to freedom of association includes the right to determine its own membership in accordance with its rules and objects.
leadingCases.significance: Led directly to EA 2008 s.19, which amended TULRCA 1992 to enable unions to expel members of political parties whose views are incompatible with the union's objects, subject to safeguards.

sections.title: Repeal of statutory procedures, restoration of Polkey, establishment of ACAS Code mechanism
sections.summary: The EA 2008 Part 1 enacted the recommendations of the Gibbons Review by repealing the failed EA 2002 statutory dispute resolution procedures and replacing them with a principles-based framework centred on the ACAS Code of Practice. This was a legislative correction — an admission that the statutory procedures had made things WORSE. The provisions restored the Polkey principle, established the 25% ACAS Code adjustment mechanism, and reformed ACAS conciliation.
sections.text:
THE LEGISLATIVE CORRECTION — WHAT THE EA 2008 DID:

(1) s.1 — REPEAL OF STATUTORY PROCEDURES:
Repealed EA 2002 ss.29-33 and Schedules 2-4 in their ENTIRETY. This removed:
(a) The mandatory 3-step Disciplinary and Dismissal Procedure (DDP).
(b) The mandatory 3-step Grievance Procedure (GP).
(c) The modified 2-step procedures for gross misconduct and former employees.
(d) The requirement that the statutory procedures were an IMPLIED TERM of every employment contract.
(e) EA 2002 s.31 — the mandatory 10-50% award adjustment for failure to follow statutory procedures.
(f) EA 2002 s.32 — the prohibition on presenting certain tribunal complaints until Step 1 of the grievance procedure had been completed and 28 days had elapsed.
Effect: from 6 April 2009, the statutory procedures ceased to exist. Transitional provisions applied to disputes that arose before that date.

(2) s.2 — RESTORATION OF POLKEY:
Repealed ERA 1996 s.98A (inserted by EA 2002). s.98A had provided that:
(a) A dismissal was automatically unfair if the employer failed to follow the statutory DDP.
(b) BUT: if the employer followed the statutory procedure, any other procedural failures would be DISREGARDED if they 'would have made no difference to the outcome'.

The repeal of s.98A RESTORED the Polkey principle: procedural fairness matters independently of outcome. A dismissal can be unfair purely because the employer failed to follow a fair procedure, even if the employee would have been dismissed anyway. However, compensation can be reduced (the 'Polkey reduction') to reflect the chance the employee would have been dismissed in any event.

This is one of the most important legislative corrections in UK employment law. The partial reversal of Polkey by s.98A had WEAKENED unfair dismissal protection — employers could get away with poor procedures if they followed the statutory minimum. The EA 2008 restored the expectation of GENUINE procedural fairness.

(3) s.3 — ACAS CODE 25% ADJUSTMENT MECHANISM:
Amended TULRCA 1992 to create a new mechanism:
(a) Where a relevant ACAS Code of Practice applies (specifically, the Code on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures), and
(b) A party has UNREASONABLY failed to follow the Code, and
(c) The matter has come before an employment tribunal,
The tribunal has DISCRETION to adjust the award by up to 25% — upward if the employer failed, downward if the employee failed.

This replaced the MANDATORY 10-50% adjustment under EA 2002 s.31 with a DISCRETIONARY 25% adjustment. Key differences:
— Discretionary not mandatory — the tribunal assesses whether adjustment is 'just and equitable'.
— 25% not 50% — the maximum is lower, reflecting the proportionality principle.
— Based on UNREASONABLE failure — not every departure triggers adjustment, only unreasonable ones.
— Applies to the ACAS Code — a principles-based document, not rigid statutory steps.

THIS MECHANISM REMAINS IN FORCE AS AT MARCH 2026 AND IS NOT BEING CHANGED BY THE ERA 2025.

(4) ss.4-7 — TRIBUNAL AND CONCILIATION REFORMS:
(a) s.4: Tribunals can reach determinations WITHOUT A HEARING if both parties consent and the tribunal considers it appropriate.
(b) ss.5-6: ACAS conciliation reformed — Acas's duty to conciliate continues throughout proceedings (no longer limited to a fixed period). The duty to seek reinstatement/re-engagement became discretionary rather than mandatory.
(c) s.7: Tribunals can award compensation for FINANCIAL LOSS in monetary claims (not just recovery of the amount owed).

HOW THIS FRAMEWORK LIVES TODAY (March 2026):

The EA 2008 dispute resolution framework is the GOVERNING FRAMEWORK for disciplinary, dismissal, and grievance disputes in 2026:

— The POLKEY principle governs procedural fairness in all dismissal cases.
— The ACAS Code of Practice (revised 2015) sets out the expected standards.
— The 25% adjustment mechanism incentivises compliance.
— The ERA 2025 does NOT replace this framework. It BUILDS ON IT by:
  (a) Extending tribunal time limits from 3 to 6 months (October 2026).
  (b) Removing the compensatory award cap for unfair dismissal (January 2027).
  (c) Reducing the qualifying period for unfair dismissal to 6 months (January 2027).
  (d) Extending ACAS early conciliation to 12 weeks (already in effect, December 2025).
  (e) Establishing the Fair Work Agency for coordinated enforcement (April 2026).

The lesson: the EA 2008 framework WORKS. The ERA 2025 strengthens individual rights and enforcement within that framework — it does not dismantle it.
sections.keyPrinciple: The EA 2008 is a corrective Act that restored balance to UK dispute resolution law. The Polkey principle is the cornerstone: procedure matters. The ACAS Code is the guide: principles not prescriptions. The 25% mechanism is the incentive: follow the Code or face adjustment. The ERA 2025 does not replace this — it builds on it. Eileen must understand this framework to advise on ANY disciplinary, dismissal, or grievance situation.
refs: Polkey v AE Dayton Services [1988] AC 344 (restored by EA 2008 s.2)
ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)
EA 2002 ss.29-33, Schs.2-4 (repealed by EA 2008 s.1)
ERA 2025 — extending time limits, removing compensation cap, reducing qualifying period

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The ACAS Code is the CENTRAL document in UK dispute resolution. It replaced the failed statutory procedures. Failure to follow it can result in a 25% adjustment to tribunal awards. Every employer should have a copy and every manager should understand it.

commonErrors: Not understanding that the ACAS Code replaced mandatory statutory procedures — some employers still believe there is a statutory 3-step process. There is not. The ACAS Code is principles-based and flexible. But failure to follow it has real consequences (25% adjustment)

Treating the ACAS Code as optional because it is 'non-statutory' — it is not optional in practice. Tribunals MUST take it into account. Unreasonable failure to follow it can result in a 25% uplift to the employee's award. In a large unfair dismissal claim, 25% of the compensatory award can be a significant sum

Not understanding the Polkey principle — procedure matters independently of outcome. Even if the employee would have been dismissed anyway, failure to follow a fair procedure makes the dismissal unfair. Compensation may be reduced (the Polkey reduction) but the dismissal is still unfair and the employer still faces a tribunal claim

Applying rigid procedural steps rather than the principles in the ACAS Code — the lesson of the EA 2002 failure is that rigid prescriptive procedures INCREASE conflict. The ACAS Code approach (principles-based, flexible, proportionate to the size and resources of the employer) is the model to follow

Not knowing that the ERA 2025 builds on this framework without replacing it — the ACAS Code mechanism, the Polkey principle, and the 25% adjustment all continue under the ERA 2025. The ERA 2025 changes relate to qualifying periods, compensation caps, time limits, and enforcement — not the dispute resolution framework itself

parts.title: Part 2 — NMW and Agency Enforcement: Evolution to the Fair Work Agency (ss.8-18)

sections.title: National Minimum Wage enforcement, employment agency enforcement, and their consolidation into the Fair Work Agency
sections.summary: The EA 2008 strengthened both NMW and employment agency enforcement. These provisions are being CONSOLIDATED into the Fair Work Agency (FWA) from April 2026 under the ERA 2025. The FWA represents the EVOLUTIONARY SUCCESSOR to the separate enforcement regimes — bringing NMW, holiday pay, statutory payments, and agency standards under a single coordinated body.
sections.text:
NMW ENFORCEMENT (ss.8-12):

(1) s.8 — ARREARS CALCULATION:
Introduced a new method of calculating NMW arrears that uses the CURRENT minimum wage rate (not the rate at the time of underpayment). This means arrears increase as the minimum wage increases — a stronger deterrent.

(2) s.9 — SINGLE NOTICE OF UNDERPAYMENT:
Replaced the previous system of separate enforcement and penalty notices with a single 'notice of underpayment'. The notice:
(a) Specifies the amount of arrears owed.
(b) Requires the employer to pay the arrears within a specified period.
(c) Includes a civil PENALTY — originally up to £5,000, subsequently increased to £20,000 per worker (from March 2014).

(3) s.10 — ENHANCED INSPECTION POWERS:
NMW enforcement officers can now REMOVE documents (not just inspect and copy them) provided they return them as soon as reasonably practicable. This prevents employers from destroying evidence.

(4) s.11 — EITHER-WAY OFFENCES:
NMW offences can now be tried in EITHER the magistrates' court or the Crown Court, enabling more serious penalties for systematic underpayment.

(5) ss.12-14 — SPECIFIC EXCLUSIONS:
Cadet Force Adult Volunteers and genuine voluntary workers excluded from NMW.

EMPLOYMENT AGENCY ENFORCEMENT (ss.15-18):

(6) s.15 — EITHER-WAY OFFENCES:
Offences under the Employment Agencies Act 1973 made triable in either court.

(7) s.16 — ENHANCED INSPECTION POWERS:
Employment Agency Standards Inspectorate (EASI) given additional inspection powers.

(8) s.17 — SCOTTISH PARTNERSHIPS:
Partners personally liable where offences committed by Scottish partnerships.

(9) s.18 — INFORMATION SHARING:
NMW enforcement officers and EASI inspectors can share information for their respective enforcement functions. This broke down the information silos between the two enforcement bodies.

EVOLUTION TO THE FAIR WORK AGENCY (ERA 2025, April 2026):

The separate enforcement regimes strengthened by the EA 2008 are being CONSOLIDATED into a single body:

The Fair Work Agency (FWA) — established 7 April 2026:
(a) ABSORBS enforcement functions from HMRC (NMW), EASI (employment agencies), and other bodies.
(b) COORDINATED enforcement across NMW, holiday pay, statutory payments (SSP, SMP, etc.), and employment agency standards.
(c) Power to INSPECT business premises and documentation.
(d) Power to issue CIVIL PENALTIES for non-compliance.
(e) Matthew Taylor CBE (author of the Taylor Review 2017) appointed as first chair.

The FWA represents the logical conclusion of the EA 2008's enforcement philosophy: stronger, more coordinated enforcement of basic employment standards. The EA 2008 strengthened individual enforcement regimes. The ERA 2025 unifies them.

IMPLICATIONS FOR EMPLOYERS:
(a) Expect MORE PROACTIVE enforcement — the FWA will inspect, not just respond to complaints.
(b) Ensure NMW compliance is AUDITABLE — the FWA can request records and impose penalties.
(c) Agency users: ensure your agency suppliers comply with the Conduct Regulations — the FWA will enforce against agencies AND hirers.
(d) The £20,000 per-worker civil penalty for NMW underpayment remains. The FWA will have the full range of EA 2008 enforcement powers plus additional ERA 2025 powers.
sections.keyPrinciple: The EA 2008 enforcement provisions were a stepping stone to the Fair Work Agency. The philosophy is consistent: employers who underpay workers or breach employment agency standards should face meaningful consequences. The EA 2008 gave enforcement officers better tools. The ERA 2025 gives those tools to a single, coordinated body with the mandate and resources to use them proactively.
refs: Employment Agencies Act 1973 (as amended by EA 2008 ss.15-17)
Conduct of Employment Agencies and Employment Businesses Regulations 2003
commonErrors: Believing that NMW enforcement is purely complaint-driven — the FWA (from April 2026) will conduct PROACTIVE inspections. Employers should ensure compliance before an inspection arrives, not after

Not knowing that the civil penalty for NMW underpayment is £20,000 PER WORKER — for an employer underpaying 50 workers, the penalty can be up to £1,000,000. Plus the arrears owed. Plus naming and shaming

Assuming the EA 2008 enforcement provisions have been superseded by the ERA 2025 — they have not. The EA 2008 powers continue. The FWA inherits and EXTENDS them. The existing enforcement framework is being consolidated, not replaced

Not ensuring that employment agency suppliers comply with the Conduct Regulations — the FWA will enforce against agencies. But hirers who knowingly use non-compliant agencies face reputational and operational risk

versions.text: SEPARATE ENFORCEMENT: NMW enforced by HMRC, employment agency standards enforced by EASI. EA 2008 provisions strengthened both. Information sharing enabled but enforcement remained siloed.
versions.policyRationale: The Brown Government strengthened enforcement tools but did not consolidate the enforcement bodies. This left gaps in coordination that the Taylor Review (2017) later identified.

versions.text: CONSOLIDATED ENFORCEMENT: Fair Work Agency established, absorbing NMW, agency, and other enforcement functions into a single coordinated body. EA 2008 enforcement powers continue within the FWA framework.
versions.amendedBy: Employment Rights Act 2025 (Fair Work Agency provisions)
versions.policyRationale: The Starmer Government's stated objective: a single enforcement body with the mandate, resources, and powers to ensure employers comply with basic employment standards. Proactive enforcement, not just complaint-driven investigation.

pendingAmendments.instrument: Fair Work Agency — full enforcement powers (date TBC, after April 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: The FWA is established 7 April 2026 but will not begin exercising its full enforcement powers until a date yet to be confirmed. Monitor for the commencement of enforcement powers.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — proactive enforcement will change the compliance landscape

pendingAmendments.instrument: ACAS Code of Practice — potential review
pendingAmendments.status: The Government has indicated it may review the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures. No timetable confirmed. The current Code (2015) remains in force.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — any revision to the Code would require policy and training updates

pendingAmendments.instrument: Tribunal time limits — extension to 6 months (October 2026)
pendingAmendments.status: ERA 2025 extends tribunal time limits from 3 months to 6 months for most claims. This does not change the EA 2008 framework but affects the TIMING of claims within it.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — employers face a longer exposure window for potential claims

surveillanceStatus.status: IN FORCE — enforcement provisions being consolidated into Fair Work Agency
